pages.text:
Support from
Student Advocacy
Dr Ash McIntyre
Student Advocate

What We Do
- Advise on University policy, procedure and practice
- We prioritise a student-led practice
- Free and confidential advice for all university rules and regulations
- Advice and support for students
- We are not decision makers
Consider: What are your
expectations of the HDR experience?

How to set shared expectations
In our experience, most issues that arise during candidature are a result of a misalignment of expectations.
- Find balance between yours and your supervisors’ expectations from the beginning of candidature:
- Project expectations
- Expectations around submitting work, getting feedback, and research guidance
- Confirm these in writing
- Graduate Research supervisor/ student expectations
- Send notes after meetings to confirm key discussion/ action points
- As soon as you experience any doubts or concerns, raise them – don’t let your concerns sit
- Communication is key!
Consider:
If you had an issue you wanted to raise, how would you do it?
If someone had an issue with you, how would you want them to raise it?

Raising Concerns/ Having Difficult Conversations
Raising concerns can be difficult! But often times, it is easier to have the hard conversation early, rather than
waiting to see what happens, ESPECIALLY when your time is limited by your candidature
1- Choose the right time and place
2- Be prepared
3- Be specific and factual
4- Use “I” statements
5- Stay calm and professional
6- Propose Solutions
7- Listen actively to your supervisor’s response
8- Follow up
9- Seek further support if needed
• Resources on the counselling website:
https://www.newcastle.edu.au/current-students/support

Setting Your Expectations
As part of this Weeks activity, you need to complete the supervisory Expectations form with your supervisor.
Let’s explore these statements in line with your expectations:
- The supervisors are responsible for determining the research topic.
- At the commencement of the research, the supervisors should direct the programme.
- The supervisors should ultimately decide the theoretical approach of the student’s research.
- It is the supervisors’ responsibility to assist students with written expression
- Scheduling meetings is the responsibility of the supervisors.
- The supervisors should proactively monitor the progress of the student.
- It is the supervisors’ responsibility to detect when a student may be at risk of non-completion.
- It is the supervisors’ prerogative to terminate a student’s project if it appears beyond the ability of the
student.

When in doubt…
- Refer to the policy: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR HIGHER DEGREE BY RESEARCH CANDIDATURE
- This outlines both the candidates' responsibilities and the supervisors’ responsibilities, in
addition to the responsibilities of Schools, Colleges and the University
And remember, this is a human relationship! Communication is a slippery thing, and
messages get lost in translation. Many issues that arise can be solved with clear
communication, re-assessing expectations, and respecting boundaries.
My key takeaway? Don’t be afraid to have a conversation!

Ways Advocacy Can Help
- Act as a support person for you in meetings
- If you are raising a concern or are feeling nervous about a meeting, we can sit in
as a support person
- Act as a guide in understanding policy and process, and help confirm general
expectations around HDR candidature
- Support you in liaising with GR, counselling and other relevant services
- Support the preparation of Leave of Absence statements, extension statements etc.
- If something is concerning you, but you aren’t sure who you should
speak to, try us!

Respectful Supervisory Relationships
CONTENT CAUTION
Topics which may come up in today’s session:
- Sex and sexual acts
- Sexual harm
- Emotional, verbal and physical abuse
Please feel free to leave the session if you find the content distressing and reach out for
support. Please note a staff member may follow up to check on your welfare afterwards.

Prevention
RESPECTFUL
COMMUNITIES Education
Culture change
WHAT WE DO
Student voice
Jayne McCartney
Respectful Communities Coordinator

Relevant Guidelines and Policies
▪ Code of Conduct
▪ Student Conduct Rule
▪ Conflict of Interest Policy
▪ Respect @ Work
▪ Sexual Harassment, Sex-based Harassment and Sexual Assault Prevention & Response Policy
and Procedure
▪ Universities Australia - Principles For Respectful Supervisory Relationships
“Universities have legal, regulatory and ethical obligations to provide a high-quality supportive
and safe learning environment for their students. Our organisations recognise that there is a
power imbalance between students and their supervisors, and a sexual or romantic relationship
that occurs within this power imbalance raises serious questions about capacity for consent and
academic integrity.”

• A sexual or romantic relationship
WHAT'S
between a supervisor and their
student is never appropriate.
NOT OK
• Sexual assault and sexual
harassment are unacceptable.

• Boundaries
WHAT
• Expectations
IS OK • Support
• Clear communication

Sexual Harassment: What it Looks Like
Sexual harassment means any unwelcome sexual behaviour that a reasonable person could
anticipate may make another person feel offended, intimidated or humiliated in that situation.
▪ Sexual harassment is not always obvious. It includes a wide range of behaviours. The most
common behaviours are:
▪ Sexually suggestive comments or jokes that offend or intimidate
▪ Intrusive questions about a person’s private life or physical appearance
▪ Sharing sexual stories or pornographic material without consent
▪ Inappropriate staring or leering
▪ Inappropriate physical contact
▪ Unwelcome touching, hugging, cornering or kissing
